naturalLanguage: A log cabin is a kind of building -- a structure providing shelter and enclosing space for human habitation or use.
A log cabin is a stationary artifact: a manufactured physical object fixed in place, constructed from natural timber materials.
Building a log cabin is a construction process: a deliberate sequence of activities that transforms raw timber into a functional shelter structure.

Appalachian log cabin construction is a heritage skill encoding generational knowledge of timber selection, notching techniques, and construction sequencing specific to the central and southern Appalachian tradition.
An Anishinaabe wigwam is a kind of building -- a constructed shelter providing protection from weather and a defined interior space for habitation.
Wigwam construction is an Anishinaabe heritage skill, encoding knowledge of sapling selection, bending and lashing techniques, and covering materials appropriate to season and location.
A Haudenosaunee longhouse is a kind of building -- a long, barrel-vaulted structure housing multiple family units within a shared interior space organized along a central aisle.

A catenary dome shelter -- whether igloo, wigwam, or quinzhee -- is a stationary artifact whose structural stability derives from the catenary arch: the natural curve of a chain hanging under its own weight, inverted as a compression arch.
An Inuit tupiq (summer tent) is a kind of building -- a portable, skin-covered shelter supported by a framework of driftwood or whalebone poles, designed for seasonal use on the land.

Traditional shelter construction across Appalachian, First Nations, and Inuit traditions is a heritage skill: knowledge-intensive activities transmitted through apprenticeship and community practice across generations.
